coredata.dc:title: Silver nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots based Pectin films: A unique fluorescent sensor for the detection of Tartrazine in candy samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Joel Amalan, A.
Gopika Devi, S.
Pius, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: This research work demonstrates an easy synthesis of Silver nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots-based Pectin films (AgNPs@BCDs/PN) and its use towards sensitive and selective detection of Tartrazine (TZ) in candy samples by spectrofluorimetry method. Blue Carbon Dots were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of Citric acid and Thiourea. AgNPs@BCDs were prepared using mechanical stirring. The particle sizes of AgNPs@BCDs were found to be in the range of 12.5 to 23.8 nm. Various techniques were deployed to explore the structural, morphological and photoluminescent behaviour of AgNPs@BCDs. The fluorescence method achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 21.02 nM within the range of 0–32 μM. Combination of Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) and Surface state modulation was adopted for the sensing. The recoveries and relative standard deviations were 94.11–99.42 % and 1.61–1.91 % respectively. This work highlights the great application prospects of AgNPs@BCDs/PN for Tartrazine sensing in a rapid, simple and sensitive way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food additives
Blue Carbon Dots (BCDs)
Photoinduced Electron Transfer
Surface state modification
Fluorescence quenching
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Tartrazine using AgNPs@BCDs and AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film sensor
Film based sensor for the detection of Tartrazine
Determination of Tartrazine in candy samples
Advantages and limitations of the AgNPs@BCDs/PN Sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of a) BCDs, b) AgNPs, c) AgNPs@BCDs and d) Silver Nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots-based Pectin films (AgNPs@BCDs/PN); Inset: AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film in normal light.
a) HR-TEM images, b) lattice spacing c) SAED pattern & d) average particle size of AgNPs@BCDs.
(a) UV–Visible absorption and fluorescence emission of BCDs (green and pink) and AgNPs@BCDs (red and blue) respectively, (b) various excitation wavelengths of AgNPs@BCDs.

(a) UV–Visible absorption spectra of AgNPs@BCDs and AgNPs@BCDs + Tartrazine and Tartrazine; (b)The fluorescence spectra of AgNPs@BCDs upon addition of different concentration of Tartrazine; (c) Linear calibration plot for Tartrazine and (d) Fluorescence behaviour of AgNPs@BCDs on interacting with various dye molecules, cations and anions.
Plausible mechanisms (a) Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) and (b) Surface state modulation for the sensing of Tartrazine using AgNPs@BCDs.

a) the fluorescence spectra of AgNPs@BCDs/PN in the absence of Tartrazine, b) presence of 32 μM Tartrazine (inset: dried films under fluorescent light), c) Fluorescent intensities of bare Pectin films, AgNPs@BCDs/PN based films in the absence and presence of Tartrazine.
Determination of Tartrazine in candy samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a = value = mean (n = 3); b = Not detectable.

head.ce:title: Silver nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots based Pectin films: A unique fluorescent sensor for the detection of Tartrazine in candy samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University, Gandhigram, Dindigul 624 302 Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University, Gandhigram, Dindigul 624 302, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research work demonstrates an easy synthesis of Silver nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots-based Pectin films (AgNPs@BCDs/PN) and its use towards sensitive and selective detection of Tartrazine (TZ) in candy samples by spectrofluorimetry method. Blue Carbon Dots were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of Citric acid and Thiourea. AgNPs@BCDs were prepared using mechanical stirring. The particle sizes of AgNPs@BCDs were found to be in the range of 12.5 to 23.8 nm. Various techniques were deployed to explore the structural, morphological and photoluminescent behaviour of AgNPs@BCDs. The fluorescence method achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 21.02 nM within the range of 0–32 μM. Combination of Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) and Surface state modulation was adopted for the sensing. The recoveries and relative standard deviations were 94.11–99.42 % and 1.61–1.91 % respectively. This work highlights the great application prospects of AgNPs@BCDs/PN for Tartrazine sensing in a rapid, simple and sensitive way.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The problem of food safety has become more prominent in recent years because people worry about additives in food and possible contamination [1]. Various food products such as beverages, wine, jellies, cakes and candies contain food additives including Tartrazine [2], Sunset yellow [3], Monosodium Glutamate [4], and artificial sweeteners like Aspartame [5,6] and Saccharin [7]. The synthetic food pigment Tartrazine commonly referred to as lemon yellow, with an IUPAC name of 1-(4-sulfonic acid phenyl)-4-(4-sulfonic acid phenyl azo)-5-pyrazolone-3-carboxylic acid trisodium salt [8–10] remains banned from processed foods as well as meat and fish. Various foods contain an approved World Health Organization level of 7.5 mg/kg body weight per day [11]. Both excessive use and one-time consumption can lead to diarrhoea, allergies, migraines, blurred vision, and severe damage to kidneys and liver[10,12,13]. The need exists for developing an affordable yet effective method to detect Tartrazine.

Commonly used techniques for the detection of Tartrazine in food products are said to be high-performance liquid chromatography [14], electrochemical analysis [15,16], capillary electrophoresis [17] and spectrophotometry [18]. Due to its ease of sample preparation, low cost, low toxicity, quick reaction, and high sensitivity, fluorescence sensing approaches for food contaminants have been drawing interest from researchers worldwide. Scientists have grown interested in Carbon Dots (CDs), a group of nano-carbons consisting of quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with a diameter less than 10 nm [19–21], due to their interesting properties. [22,23]. Much effort has gone into improvising the functional properties of CD scaffolds, expanding their uses in drug delivery, sensing, imaging and optoelectronic devices. [24 21,25,26]. Due to their unique property, CDs can be surface- tailored more easily with other dopants [27]. Doping Carbon Dots with hetero-atoms enhances fluorescence efficiency and sensing selectivity [28]. Among the dopants, Nitrogen doping is popular due to high fluorescence yield [29,30]. Sulfur doping optimizes the electrical structure of carbon dots [31]. Adding of sulphur enhances the “N” states and prevent “O” states from rising [32]. Metal nanoparticles are popular in various application domains, including industry, medicine, and catalysis. Of all the metal nanoparticles, Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) may be a better fit for the development of sensors because of their affordability, ease of use, and excellent sensitivity [33]. AgNPs have very uncommon optical features such a high molar extinction coefficient, a tunable absorption wavelength, and an enhanced Raman scattering effect because of their distinct Local Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) [34,35].

Biopolymers such as proteins, lipids and polysaccharides are being considered for Carbon Dots use because of their biodegradability, sustainability and enhanced film-forming properties [36]. Pectin (PN), a water-soluble plant heteropolysaccharide that is acidic, is utilized for making composite films and is popular in the food industry [37]. It has been utilized to create biopolymer-based composite films and primarily consists of a linear chain of α-(1 − 4)-linked D-galacturonic acid units with different amounts of methoxyl or methyl ester. Its non-toxic, biocompatible, and biodegradable state makes it a viable biopolymer for sensing applications [38,39].

These CD-based nano-probes are often dispersed and kept in a liquid media, making them inconvenient for long-term storage and transfer. Furthermore, due to the energy transmission between neighbouring CD particles, these nanoprobes are quenched by aggregation. These limitations hinder the technical uses of CD-based nanoprobes, including sensing. One intriguing approach to resolve the aforementioned issues is to disperse CDs in a solid matrix to create a composite material [38]. In this study, a novel Silver nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots encapsuled in Pectin-based films was synthesized to detect Tartrazine levels. Initially, BCDs were synthesized via simple hydrothermal method, followed by decorating AgNPs over BCDs via mechanical stirring to get AgNPs@BCDs. The size and size distribution of AgNPs@BCDs was measured using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The fluorescence emission of the synthesized material was recorded with a spectrofluorometer. Finally, the practical application of AgNPs@BCDs/PN based sensor was demonstrated by determining Tartrazine in candy samples.

Analytical grade compounds like Citric acid (C6H8O7), Thiourea (CH4N2S), Silver Nitrate (AgNO3), Trisodium Citrate (Na3C6H5O7), Sodium Borohydride (NaBH4) and Pectin (C47H68O16) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and were used without further purification.

BCDs were synthesized using simple hydrothermal method.[40] (Fig. 1 .a). 2 g Citric acid (C6H8O7) and 2 g Thiourea (CH4N2S) were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water (DI) and sonicated for about 5 min. After that, the resultant mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. The resulting solution was collected, diluted with ethanol and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The purified BCDs were stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

AgNPs solution was prepared according to the procedure mentioned in the literature [26] (Fig. 1.b). Silver Nitrate and Trisodium Citrate solutions of equal molar ratio were taken in a round bottom flask and stirred vigorously at 20 °C. Then, 1 mL of freshly prepared Sodium Borohydride solution was added dropwise and the solution was again subjected to vigorous stirring for 2 h at room temperature. AgNPs were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, to remove excess stabilizing agents and unreacted Silver precursors with deionized water. The resulting yellow solution indicated the formation of AgNPs and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

A round-bottom flask was filled with 5 mL of BCD solution, and 20 mL of the AgNP solution which was prepared (in a 1:4 ratio) was quickly added. The mixture was then stirred for 30 min at room temperature. The color of the solution turned from yellow to brownish yellow (Fig. 1.c). The solution was stored again at 4 °C for further investigations. [26].

Pectin films were prepared using sol–gel technique. 2 g Pectin was mixed vigorously with 100 mL deionized water for 1 h at room temperature. Once the solution became homogenous, it was poured into a petri dish to obtain Pectin based films [38].

Preparation of AgNPs@BCDs/PN films was done according to a reported literature with a small alteration[38] (Fig. 1.d). Pectin solution was prepared and known amount of AgNPs@BCDs solution was poured into the Pectin solution and stirred vigorously and once the AgNPs@BCDs/PN solution becomes homogenous, the solution is poured into a petri dish and allowed to dry at room temperature for the formation of AgNPs@BCDs/PN film.

A JASCO V-750 UV–Visible spectrometer was used to detect the absorption spectra. At room temperature, fluorescence spectrum measurements were performed using a JASCO FP-8500 spectrofluorimeter fitted with a xenon discharge lamp. The JASCO FT-IR 460 plus model was used to record the FT-IR measurements. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of AgNPs@BCDs were obtained from a JEOL JEM 2100 Advanced Analytical HR-TEM, operating at 200 kV. The Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern was collected using a PANanalytical Xpert 3 equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Tartrazine using AgNPs@BCDs and AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A range of Tartrazine solution concentrations were added to the cuvette after 50 µL of the AgNPs@BCDs was dissolved in 1500 µL of water (pH = 7) for Tartrazine detection. After mixing the solutions for a minute, at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 328 nm and an emission wavelength of 403 nm was measured.

To detect Tartrazine using an AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film sensor, a 2 cm × 1 cm measured film was cut with a surface area that could readily hold a standard 5 mL vial. Due to the film’s water stability, it was immersed in 1.5 mL of deionised water. Following the addition of range of Tartrazine solution concentrations, the vials were examined for fluorescence quenching under both normal and fluorescent lightings. Following an examination for fluorescence quenching, the films were dried in a hot air oven. They were subsequently processed through spectrofluorimetry, which recorded the emission wavelength at 403 nm at an excitation wavelength of 328 nm.

Fig. 2 . shows the PXRD pattern of AgNPs@BCDs. It showed a broad peak at 26.85°, which is attributed to their Graphitic components (JCPDS No. 75-0444) [41]. In Fig. 3 . (a-d), HR-TEM image, lattice spacing, SAED pattern and average particle size of AgNPs@BCDs are displayed. The HRTEM image of BCDs and AgNPs typically show well dispersed with nearly spherical shape and quasi-spherical shape, respectively (Fig. S1). AgNPs are well decorated over BCDs, which is evident in Fig. 3a. BCDs cause efficient aggregation of AgNPs, as evidenced by the spherical form of AgNPs@BCDs with an average particle size of 15.487 nm as shown in Fig. 3d. The distinct lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.2936 nm is shown in Fig. 3b. Amorphous nature of the material as a whole is consistent with the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern and validates the poor crystalline nature of AgNPs@BCDs in Fig. 3c. The synthesized material contained only Carbon, Oxygen, Sulphur, Nitrogen and Silver, according to the point beam EDS analysis of AgNPs@BCDs (Fig. S2). This suggests that there was no indication of any other impurity elements in the HRTEM grid. The percentage of each constituent in the prepared material is shown in Table S1

The surface functional groups of AgNPs, BCDs, AgNPs@BCDs, bare PN film and AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film were identified using ATR-FTIR spectra, which is showed in Fig. S3. Two peaks at 3305 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 in the AgNPs (black) FTIR spectra indicate the OH and C=O stretching vibrations [26]. Five prominent peaks were clearly visible in the BCDs (blue) FTIR spectra at 3345 cm−1, 1633 cm−1, 1498 cm−1, 1366 cm−1 and 1158 cm−1. These peaks corresponded to the stretching vibrations of –OH, −C=O, −C=N, and −C-S, respectively. Furthermore, AgNPs are present over the BCDs, as evidenced by the six peaks in the FTIR spectrum of AgNPs@BCDs (dark yellow) at 3331 cm−1, 2120 cm−1, 1635 cm−1, 1444 cm−1, 1368 cm−1 and 592 cm−1, which correspond to the –OH, Ag-C=O, −C=O, C–H, −C-N, and Ag-O bonds, respectively. Bare PN film (green) shows peaks at 3299 cm−1, 2926 cm−1, 1763 cm−1 and 1783.5 cm−1 which represents the OH stretching vibrations, aliphatic C–H bond, C=O bond which is present in alcohols, carboxylic acids as well as esters and the stretching vibrations of α-(1,4)-glucosidic bond, respectively [38,42]. The AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film (red) showed predominant peaks of both AgNPs@BCDs and PN at 3298 cm−1, 2928 cm−1, 2102 cm−1, 1738 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, 1433 cm−1, 1366 cm−1, 1093 cm−1 and 526 cm−1. These results clearly demonstrate that AgNPs@BCDs and AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film were successfully synthesized and have good compatibility with each other.

The optical properties of the prepared BCDs and AgNPs@BCDs were examined through UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 4 .a shows the absorption spectra as well as fluorescence spectra of BCDs and AgNPs@BCDs. The π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon atoms with aromatic or conjugated domains and the n-π* transition of C=O bonds can be attributed for the two peaks in the absorption spectrum for BCDs, which are located at 276 nm and 327 nm, respectively. [40]. The maximum emission was observed at 424 nm when BCDs were excited at 350 nm. The π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 groups at 273 nm is represented by the two peaks in the absorption and fluorescence spectra of AgNPs@BCDs. The peak at 328 nm may be caused by the Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect of the Silver nanoparticles, which when excited at 328 nm exhibits a maximum PL emission at 403 nm. This suggests that the Silver nanoparticles are well-decorated over the Blue Carbon Dots. The emission spectra of AgNPs@BCDs in Fig. 4.b, shows an increasing excitation wavelength (red shift) from 310 nm to 330 nm followed by gradual decrease in intensity. This finding confirmed that the excitation wavelength significantly affects the fluorescence of AgNPs@BCDs.

The luminous properties of the synthesized AgNPs@BCDs were examined at different pH values, material concentrations, reaction times, and storage durations. As seen in Fig. S4a, AgNPs@BCDs are very stable in the pH range of 3–9. The PL intensity steadily declines at pH 9. The highest PL intensity was seen at pH 7 in the pH range of 3–9. Therefore, the ideal pH was determined to be 7. AgNPs@BCDs's concentration effect was also investigated. F0/F increased as the material's concentration grew from 0.01 to 0.05 mg/mL, as shown in Fig. S4b, and subsequently reduced as the concentration climbed from 0.05 to 0.5 mg/mL. The F0/F values did not alter significantly when the concentration was raised to 1.0 mg/mL. Consequently, the ideal concentration of AgNPs@BCDs was determined to be 0.05 mg/mL. Research was conducted to assess how reaction time affected Tartrazine detection. The AgNPs@BCDs and Tartrazine reaction were completed in less than a minute, as Fig. S4c illustrates. Finally, the optimal reaction time for Tartrazine detection was found to be one minute and this value was used for additional study. Furthermore, AgNPs@BCDs' long-term storage was examined, and as Fig. S4d illustrates, the fluorescence intensity remained constant for three months. As a result, the AgNPs@BCDs can be kept in a uniform phase without aggregating for a long time.

Under optimized conditions, AgNPs@BCDs were exposed to various concentrations of Tartrazine in order to determine the change in fluorescence intensity, which is shown in Fig. 5 . The UV absorption spectra of AgNPs@BCDs, AgNPs@BCDs + Tartrazine and Tartrazine are shown in Fig. 5.a. UV–Visible spectra showed peaks at 257 and 427 nm. AgNPs@BCDs + Tartrazine exhibited two strong absorption peaks at 265 and 423 nm. In addition, no new UV–vis absorption peak was observed for the AgNPs@BCDs + Tartrazine, revealing that there were no new compounds formed. In order to understand the interaction between Tartrazine and AgNPs@BCDs, the quenching mechanism was investigated. AgNPs@BCDs show a maximum emission at 403 nm. As the Tartrazine concentration increases, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with a gradual blue shift. As the blue shift occurs, the emission peak gradually shifts from 403 nm to 391 nm (Fig. 5.b). The reason for the simultaneous decrease in fluorescence intensity and the gradual blue shift was due to Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) [43,44] and Surface state modulation [45–47]. The calibration curve of the concentration of Tartrazine versus F0/F is shown in Fig. 5c. The fluorescence quenching efficiency showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Tartrazine in the detection range of 0–32 μM as for Tartrazine, shown in Fig. 5.c. The equation of linear regression was F0/F = 1021 + 105.28C (C is the concentration of Tartrazine, μM) with a correlation coefficient 0.904, and the detection limit was estimated to be 21.02 nM. Table S2 shows a comparison of other methods and materials used with for the detection of Tartrazine with the method discussed above and it shows that the latter one is simple, rapid and sensitive. To assess the selectivity of the system, the fluorescence response of AgNPs@BCDs with 15 substances that are normally found in candies like K+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl-, HCO3 –, Metanil Yellow (MY), Auramine-O (Au-O), Red Orange (RO), Acid Orange (AO), Sunset Yellow (SY), Malachite Green (MG), Brilliant Blue (BB), Rhodamine 6G (R6G) were taken. The concentration of Tartrazine was about 20 μM, whereas the concentrations of Metanil yellow, Red Orange, Auramine-O, Acid Orange, Sunset Yellow were 10 times the amount of Tartrazine, Malachite Green, Brilliant Blue, Rhodamine 6G were about 5 times the amount of Tartrazine. Only Tartrazine produced a distinct decrease in the PL intensity while the other substances did not affect the PL intensity as shown in Fig. 5.d. of the relative fluorescence response (F/F0 ) versus different analytes.

When Silver nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots (AgNPs@BCDs) interact with Tartrazine, fluorescence quenching and a blue shift in emission occur due to Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) and Surface state modulation as shown in Fig. 6 . Tartrazine, a food dye containing electron-withdrawing azo (−N=N-) groups, acts as an efficient electron acceptor when in proximity to excited BCDs [43,48]. Upon excitation, the electrons in the conduction band of BCDs are transferred to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of Tartrazine due to the favorable energy alignment between the two species [44,49]. This non-radiative charge transfer depletes the number of electrons available for radiative recombination in BCDs, thereby suppressing fluorescence intensity [50,51]. Additionally, Silver nanoparticles enhance this process by acting as plasmonic bridges, facilitating rapid electron migration and improving charge separation efficiency [52]. As a result, fluorescence quenching is significantly intensified due to the combination of PET and Plasmon-enhanced Energy Transfer. Beyond PET, Tartrazine also influences the surface electronic states of BCDs [45]. BCDs possess defects states or surface trap sites that contribute to lower energy (red-shifted) emissions. When Tartrazine binds to the BCD surface via electrostatic interactions or hydrogen bonding, it passivates these defect states, effectively reducing their contribution to emission [47]. Their interaction alters the electronic structure of BCDs, leading to a redistribution of available radiative pathways. Since emissions from these lower-energy trap states are surpassed, recombination predominantly occurs from higher energy states, resulting in blue shift in the fluorescent spectrum [46,53]. In simple terms, Tartrazine’s surface interaction prevents relaxation through lower-energy states, forcing the emission to originate from states associated with higher excitation energies. These two mechanisms together contribute a highly sensitive and specific response, making AgNPs@BCDs an effective sensing platform for Tartrazine detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Film based sensor for the detection of Tartrazine
ce:section.ce:para: The AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film was subjected to fluorescent detection of Tartrazine. The experiment was performed in room temperature and inside a UV chamber. As seen in Fig. S5.a and b, the prepared composite films were exposed to both normal light as well as fluorescent light. The film showed blue fluorescence in fluorescent light. Since the prepared material was stable in neutral pH of 7, they were placed in a 5 mL glass vials filled with 1.5 mL of deionized water (Fig. S5c). The films’ fluorescence was analyzed in the absence and presence of Tartrazine. AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film was subjected to the same concentration of Tartrazine in the detection range of 0–32 μM, where at 32 μM the fluorescence was quenched as shown in Fig. S5.d (right corner). The films were immediately dried at a low temperature of 40 °C and subjected to spectrofluorimetry, which is shown in Fig. 7 . a & b. Bare Pectin films do not inherently possess natural fluorescence. AgNPs@BCDs/PN based films showed maximum fluorescence in the absence of Tartrazine. But on the contrary, when exposed to Tartrazine, the AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film displayed reduced fluorescence intensity; this was confirmed in the UV chamber and spectrofluorimetry, as seen in the graph. This significant change in fluorescence intensity was plotted and shown in Fig. 7.c. From the above experiment, it was clearly evident that the prepared AgNPs@BCDs/PN based film exhibited sensitive detection of Tartrazine, which was proved by spectrofluorimetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Tartrazine in candy samples
ce:section.ce:para: Locally produced candies were utilized for real sample analysis in order to determine Tartrazine employing AgNPs@BCDs. The Samples were bought from a nearby bakery. After being weighed, the candies were made to dissolve in 100 mL of distilled water. The candies were spiked with standard solutions containing varying quantities of Tartrazine, and then tested using the suggested method. Table 1 demonstrates that the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was between 1.61 and 1.91 %, and the recoveries were within the range of 94.11 to 99.42 %, indicating great precision of the suggested procedure. All additional observations confirmed that the method elaborated in the preceding paras using AgNPs@BCDs is a viable and reliable method for determining Tartrazine in candy samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and limitations of the AgNPs@BCDs/PN Sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Among the many benefits of the developed film-based sensor are its high sensitivity, quick response time, affordability, and simplicity of setup. It is environmentally benign and appropriate for real-sample analysis due to its incorporation into a biopolymer matrix. Scalability for large-scale production, possible matrix interferences in increasingly complex food samples, and the requirement for storage stability studies are some possible drawbacks. Subsequent research endeavours can concentrate on surmounting these limitations by means of material optimization and multiplex detection techniques.

Heteroatom as well as nanoparticle doping improves the fluorescence efficiency and sensing selectivity of Carbon Dots. Silver nanoparticles decorated Blue Carbon Dots (AgNPs@BCDs) was synthesized using hydrothermal method. Characterization techniques including UV–Vis, FTIR, PXRD and HRTEM were employed to validate the successful synthesis of AgNPs@BCDs. The fluorescence properties were greatly improved by doping N, S and AgNPs. The developed approach is precise, selective, and sensitive. The fluorescence quenching of AgNPs@BCDs by Tartrazine is a result of both PET and Surface state modulation. The material was encapsulated in Pectin-based films and analysed for real time application, which showed good sensitivity over Tartrazine. Apart from good sensitivity and favoured accuracy, the additional advantages of the suggested analytical method are its rapid response, cost-effectiveness, and ease of use, making it an appealing film-based material for monitoring food additives.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge GRI-XRDPHY FACILITY funded by DST-FIST.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126304.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in Fluorescent Sensing Carbon Dots: An Account of Food Analysis
ACS Omega

Hu, Correction to: Development of a highly sensitive fluorescence method for Tartrazine determination in food matrices based on carbon dots (Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry, (2021), 413, 5, (1485-1492), 10.1007/s00216-020-03118-1), Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 413 (2021) 2275. DOI: 10.1007/s00216-021-03217-7.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sunset yellow in soft drinks based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots
Spectrochim. Acta - Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yuan, X. Zhao, M. Qiao, J. Zhu, S. Liu, J. Yang, X. Hu, Determination of sunset yellow in soft drinks based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots, Spectrochim. Acta - Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc. 167 (2016) 106–110. DOI: 10.1016/j.saa.2016.05.038.
